beginner.question: What is the <header> element in HTML5?
beginner.answer: The <header> element is a semantic HTML5 element that represents introductory content for its nearest ancestor sectioning element.

beginner.question: What kind of content goes inside a <header>?
beginner.answer: Headings (<h1>-<h6>), logos, navigation, search forms, taglines, author information, and other introductory content.

beginner.question: Can you have more than one <header> on a page?
beginner.answer: Yes. A page can have a page-level header and additional headers inside articles, sections, and asides.

beginner.question: Is <header> a block-level or inline element?
beginner.answer: <header> is a block-level element. It takes up the full width available and starts on a new line.

beginner.question: What is the difference between <header> and <head>?
beginner.answer: <head> contains meta-information about the document (title, scripts, styles) and is not displayed. <header> is a visible content element displayed at the top of a page or section.

beginner.question: Write a simple page header with a title and tagline.
Where should the page-level <header> be placed?
beginner.answer: The page-level <header> should be placed directly inside <body>, before the <main> element.

beginner.question: Can a <header> contain a <nav>?
beginner.answer: Yes, it is common and recommended to place primary navigation (<nav>) inside the <header>.

beginner.question: What is the default styling of <header>?
beginner.answer: The <header> element has no default styling beyond being a block-level element. It appears as a generic container.

beginner.question: Can <header> contain images?
beginner.answer: Yes, <header> can contain images, commonly used for logos or branding visuals.

intermediate.question: What is the implicit ARIA role of <header> and when does it apply?
intermediate.answer: When <header> is a direct child of <body>, it has an implicit ARIA role of 'banner'. Inside <article>, <section>, or <aside>, it has no implicit role.

intermediate.question: What elements cannot be placed inside a <header>?
intermediate.answer: <header> cannot contain another <header>, a <footer>, or an <address> element.

intermediate.question: How does the <header> relate to the document outline?
intermediate.answer: <header> does NOT create a new section in the document outline. It is purely a container for introductory content.

intermediate.question: Show how to create an article with a header containing title, author, and date.
Can you use <header> inside <aside>?
intermediate.answer: Yes, <aside> can contain a <header> to introduce the aside content, such as a widget heading.

intermediate.question: How do you make a sticky header that stays at the top when scrolling?
intermediate.answer: Use CSS: header { position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 100; background: white; }

intermediate.question: What is the 'skip to content' link and why is it placed in the header?
intermediate.answer: A 'skip to content' link is an accessibility feature placed at the top of the header that allows keyboard users to jump directly to the main content, bypassing navigation.

intermediate.question: Can a <header> contain a full-width background image?
intermediate.answer: Yes, you can apply a background image and cover it using CSS: header { background: url('hero.jpg') center/cover; height: 400px; }

intermediate.question: Should you put the site name in an <h1> inside the <header>?
intermediate.answer: Yes, it is common and semantically correct to put the site name in an <h1> inside the page-level header.

intermediate.question: How many HTML elements have an implicit 'banner' role?
intermediate.answer: Only the page-level <header> (direct child of <body>) has the implicit 'banner' role. No other element has this role by default.

advanced.question: What happens to the 'banner' role if <header> is not a direct child of <body>?
advanced.answer: If <header> is nested inside <article>, <section>, <aside>, or <main>, it loses the 'banner' role and becomes a generic landmark.

advanced.question: How does the <header> element interact with the Accessibility Tree in different contexts?
advanced.answer: As a page-level banner, <header> is exposed as a landmark in the accessibility tree. Section-level headers are not landmarks, so assistive technology may not navigate to them directly.

advanced.question: What is the proper way to handle multiple navigations inside a single <header>?
advanced.answer: Use multiple <nav> elements with distinct aria-label attributes (e.g., 'Main navigation', 'Language selector') inside the <header>.

advanced.question: How would you implement a 'collapsing header' that shrinks on scroll?
advanced.answer: Use JavaScript to detect scroll position and toggle CSS classes: header { transition: padding 0.3s; } .header-scrolled { padding: 5px 20px; font-size: 0.9em; }

advanced.question: What are the SEO implications of the <header> element?
advanced.answer: Search engines use <header> to understand page structure. The headings within <header> contribute to the page's content hierarchy and relevance signals.

advanced.question: Can you put <main> inside a <header>?
advanced.answer: No. <main> should not be inside <header>. <main> comes after the page-level header and contains the primary content of the page.

advanced.question: How does the <header> element differ from the CSS 'header' selector when multiple headers exist?
advanced.answer: The CSS selector 'header' matches ALL <header> elements on the page. You need to use contextual selectors (e.g., 'body > header', 'article > header') to target specific headers.

advanced.question: What is the correct way to declare the page header in a way that maximizes accessibility?
advanced.answer:
Skip to content

Site Name

...
...

advanced.question: How would you handle internationalization (i18n) content inside a <header>?
advanced.answer: Use the lang attribute on elements within the header if switching languages, or use data attributes with JavaScript for dynamic language switching.

advanced.question: What happens to <header> when printed? How would you style it for print?
advanced.answer: By default, <header> prints as it appears on screen. For print, you may want to hide navigation and CTAs: @media print { header nav, header .cta { display: none; } }
